name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ عِلْمًا ۖ وَقَالَا ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٥)
```

```english
Certainly, We gave knowledge to David and Solomon, and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who granted us an advantage over many of His faithful servants.’ (15)
```

```arabic
وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ دَاوُۥدَ ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ ٱلطَّيْرِ وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٦)
```

```english
Solomon inherited from David, and he said, ‘O people! We have been taught the speech of the birds, and we have been given out of everything. Indeed this is a manifest advantage.’ (16)
```

```arabic
وَحُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ جُنُودُهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلطَّيْرِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
[Once] Solomon’s hosts were marched out for him, comprising jinn, humans and birds, and they were held in check. (17)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ وَادِ ٱلنَّمْلِ قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّمْلُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مَسَـٰكِنَكُمْ لَا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
When they came to the Valley of Ants, an ant said, ‘O ants! Enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts should trample on you while they are unaware.’ (18)
```

```arabic
فَتَبَسَّمَ ضَاحِكًا مِّن قَوْلِهَا وَقَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَدْخِلْنِى بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِى عِبَادِكَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
Whereat he smiled, amused at its words, and he said, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which please You, and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants.’ (19)
```

```arabic
وَتَفَقَّدَ ٱلطَّيْرَ فَقَالَ مَا لِىَ لَآ أَرَى ٱلْهُدْهُدَ أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَآئِبِينَ (٢٠)
```

```english
[One day] he reviewed the birds, and said, ‘Why do I not see the hoopoe? Or is he absent?’ (20)
```

```arabic
لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُۥ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا أَوْ لَأَا۟ذْبَحَنَّهُۥٓ أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّى بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢١)
```

```english
‘I will punish him with a severe punishment, or I will behead him, unless he brings me a credible excuse.’ (21)
```

```arabic
فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ فَقَالَ أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍۭ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ (٢٢)
```

```english
He did not stay for long [before he turned up] and said, ‘I have alighted on something which you have not alighted on, and I have brought you from Sheba a definite report. (22)
```

```arabic
إِنِّى وَجَدتُّ ٱمْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
I found a woman ruling over them, and she has been given everything, and she has a great throne. (23)
```

```arabic
وَجَدتُّهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah, and Satan has made their deeds seem decorous to them—thus he has barred them from the way [of Allah], so they are not guided— (24)
```

```arabic
أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى يُخْرِجُ ٱلْخَبْءَ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُخْفُونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
so that they do not prostrate themselves to Allah, who brings forth the hidden in the heavens and the earth, and He knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose. (25)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ ۩ (٢٦)
```

```english
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Lord of the Great Throne.’ (26)
```

```arabic
۞ قَالَ سَنَنظُرُ أَصَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘We shall see whether you are truthful, or if you are one of the liars. (27)
```

```arabic
ٱذْهَب بِّكِتَـٰبِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then draw away from them and observe what [response] they return.’ (28)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ إِنِّىٓ أُلْقِىَ إِلَىَّ كِتَـٰبٌ كَرِيمٌ (٢٩)
```

```english
She said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Indeed a noble letter has been delivered to me. (29)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ مِن سُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَإِنَّهُۥ بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٣٠)
```

```english
It is from Solomon, and it begins in the name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (30)
```

```arabic
أَلَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَىَّ وَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
[It states,] ‘‘Do not defy me, and come to me in submission.’’ ’ (31)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَفْتُونِى فِىٓ أَمْرِى مَا كُنتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا حَتَّىٰ تَشْهَدُونِ (٣٢)
```

```english
She said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Give me your opinion concerning my matter. I do not decide any matter until you are present.’ (32)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أُو۟لُوا۟ قُوَّةٍ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَٱلْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَٱنظُرِى مَاذَا تَأْمُرِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
They said, ‘We are powerful and possess a great might. But it is up to you to command. So consider what orders you will give.’ (33)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ إِنَّ ٱلْمُلُوكَ إِذَا دَخَلُوا۟ قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوهَا وَجَعَلُوٓا۟ أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَآ أَذِلَّةً ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
She said, ‘Indeed when kings enter a town, they devastate it, and make the mightiest of its people the weakest. That is how they act. (34)
```

```arabic
وَإِنِّى مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِم بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَنَاظِرَةٌۢ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
I will send them a gift, and see what the envoys bring back.’ (35)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ قَالَ أَتُمِدُّونَنِ بِمَالٍ فَمَآ ءَاتَىٰنِۦَ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّآ ءَاتَىٰكُم بَلْ أَنتُم بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
So when he came to Solomon, he said, ‘Are you aiding me with wealth? What Allah has given me is better than what He has given you. Indeed, you are proud of your gift! (36)
```

```arabic
ٱرْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بِهَا وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَآ أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Go back to them, for we will come at them with hosts which they cannot face, and we will expel them from it, abased and degraded.’ (37)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِى بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ (٣٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Which of you will bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?’ (38)
```

```arabic
قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن تَقُومَ مِن مَّقَامِكَ ۖ وَإِنِّى عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِىٌّ أَمِينٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
An afreet from among the jinn said, ‘I will bring it to you before you rise from your place. I have the power to do it and am trustworthy.’ (39)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱلَّذِى عِندَهُۥ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِندَهُۥ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّى لِيَبْلُوَنِىٓ ءَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ ۖ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّى غَنِىٌّ كَرِيمٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
The one who had knowledge of the Book said, ‘I will bring it to you in the twinkling of an eye.’ So when he saw it set near him, he said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me if I will give thanks or be ungrateful. Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful [should know that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous.’ (40)
```

```arabic
قَالَ نَكِّرُوا۟ لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِىٓ أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
He said, ‘Disguise her throne for her, so that we may see whether she is discerning or if she is one of the undiscerning ones.’ (41)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَـٰكَذَا عَرْشُكِ ۖ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ ۚ وَأُوتِينَا ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ (٤٢)
```

```english
So when she came, it was said [to her], ‘Is your throne like this one?’ She said, ‘It seems to be the same, and we were informed before it, and we had submitted.’ (42)
```

```arabic
وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٣)
```

```english
She had been barred [from the way of Allah] by what she used to worship besides Allah, for she belonged to a faithless people. (43)
```

```arabic
قِيلَ لَهَا ٱدْخُلِى ٱلصَّرْحَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَن سَاقَيْهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ صَرْحٌ مُّمَرَّدٌ مِّن قَوَارِيرَ ۗ قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٤)
```

```english
It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace.’ So when she saw it, she supposed it to be a pool of water, and she bared her shanks. He said, ‘It is a palace paved with crystal.’ She said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (44)
```

## (Commentary)

A brief account of the stories of David and Solomon (peace be upon them), containing some of the wondrous reports of Solomon regarding the kingdom Allah bestowed upon him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا — **"Certainly We gave knowledge to David and Solomon"** (27:15). The use of the indefinite form for "knowledge" (عِلْمًا) serves to magnify its significance. Among the references to the knowledge of David in His speech, the Exalted, is His saying: وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ الْخِطَابِ — **"and We gave him wisdom and a decisive judgment"** (38:20). Among the references to the knowledge of Solomon is His saying: فَفَهَّمْنَاهَا سُلَيْمَانَ وَكُلًّا آتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا — **"We gave its understanding to Solomon, and to each We gave judgment and knowledge"** (21:79); the end of this verse encompasses both of them.

His saying: وَقَالَا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي فَضَّلَنَا عَلَى كَثِيرٍ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — **"and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who granted us an advantage over many of His faithful servants’"** (27:15). The "advantage" (التفضيل) intended here is either an advantage in knowledge, as supported by the context of the verse, or an advantage in the totality of the gifts Allah specifically granted them—such as the subjugation of the mountains and birds for David, the softening of iron for him, and the granting of kingship; and the subjugation of the jinn, wild beasts, birds, and the wind for Solomon, as well as teaching him the speech of birds and granting him kingship—as necessitated by the absolute sense of "advantage."

The verse—meaning His saying: وَقَالَا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ — **"and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah...’"**—is, in any case, in the position of recounting their acknowledgment of the divine favor. It thus serves as a witness to the claim indicated by the glad tidings at the beginning of the Surah: that Allah, the Glorified, will specifically grant the believers that which will delight their eyes. Similar to this are the acknowledgments of Solomon that will follow in various places of his speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ — **"Solomon inherited from David"** (27:16), meaning he inherited his wealth and his kingdom. As for the claim of some that it refers to the inheritance of prophethood and knowledge, this is problematic. Prophethood (النبوة) does not accept inheritance because it is not subject to transfer (الانتقال). As for knowledge (العلم), although it may accept transfer through a kind of figurative extension, this is only valid regarding discursive, acquired knowledge (العلم الفكري الاكتسابي). The knowledge specific to prophets and messengers is a dignity from Allah, a bestowed gift (وهبي), and is not among that which is acquired through thought. Thus, a non-prophet may inherit knowledge from a prophet, but a prophet does not inherit his knowledge from another prophet, nor from a non-prophet.

His saying: وَقَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنْطِقَ الطَّيْرِ — **"and he said, ‘O people! We have been taught the speech of the birds’"** (27:16). The apparent context is that he (peace be upon him) is speaking with pride regarding himself and his father. This constitutes a recounting of Allah’s favor (تحديث بنعمة الله), as the Exalted said: وَأَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ — **"and as for the blessing of your Lord, proclaim it"** (93:11). As for the insistence of some exegetes that the pronouns in "we have been taught" (عُلِّمْنَا) and "we have been given" (أُوتِينَا) refer only to himself—following the custom of kings and grandees when reporting about themselves, where they speak of themselves and their servants and aides to maintain the protocol of kingship—the preceding context does not fully support this.

The word "people" (الناس) carries its apparent meaning, which is the general public gathered without distinction. The claim of some that it refers to the grandees of his kingdom or their scholars is not sound.

Speech (المنطق) or speaking (النطق), as we conventionally know it, consists of a sound or composed sounds that signify, by convention (بالوضع), intended meanings for the speaker, which is called "speech" (كلام). According to al-Raghib, it is hardly ever applied except to humans. However, the Noble Quran uses it in a broader sense: the indication of a thing toward a meaning intended for itself. The Exalted said: وَقَالُوا لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدْتُمْ عَلَيْنَا قَالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللَّهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — **"They will say to their skins, ‘Why did you testify against us?’ They will say, ‘Allah, who makes everything speak, has made us speak’"** (41:21). This is either by way of conceptual analysis (تحليل المعنى)—as the Quran uses most meanings and concepts that are restricted in common usage to physical, material instances, such as seeing (الرؤية), looking (النظر), hearing (السمع), the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), the Throne (العرش), the Pedestal (الكرسي), and others—or because the word has a more general meaning, and its restriction to humans is due to the mind's tendency toward the most frequent usage (الانصراف).

In any case, the speech of birds (منطق الطير) is that by which birds indicate their purposes to one another. What we find upon reflecting on their vital conditions is that every species or type has specific simple sounds for its specific social states, according to the variety of their gatherings—such as the state of excitement for mating, the state of competition and victory, the state of loneliness and fear, the state of supplication or seeking help, and so on. Other animals are similar to birds in this regard.

However, there should be no doubt that the "speech of birds" intended in the verse carries a more subtle and broader meaning than that.

Firstly: The context of the verse testifies that he (peace be upon him) is speaking of an exclusive matter that is not within the reach of the general public, but was attained through special divine care. This aforementioned basic level of bird speech is something anyone can observe and know.

Secondly: Because what Allah, the Exalted, recounts in the following verses regarding the dialogue between Solomon and the Hoopoe (الهدهد) involves diverse, high-level knowledge that the limited sounds we find in a hoopoe could not possibly signify with such distinctness. In the hoopoe’s speech, there is mention of Allah, the Glorified, His oneness, power, knowledge, Lordship, and His Great Throne; mention of Satan and his beautifying of deeds, guidance, and misguidance, and more. It also mentions kingship, the throne, the woman and her people, and their prostration to the sun. In Solomon’s speech, there is his command to take the letter and cast it to them, then to observe what they return with. These—as is not hidden from a researcher who delves deeply into meanings—are vast areas of knowledge with deep roots, the understanding of which depends on thousands upon thousands of pieces of information. How could a few simple sounds suffice to convey such detail?

Furthermore, there is no evidence that every sound or vocal characteristic produced by an animal in its speech is within the capacity of our senses to perceive or distinguish. This is supported by what is narrated of the ant’s speech in the following verses, which is certainly part of animal speech, yet the ant has no sound that reaches our hearing. It is also supported by what natural scientists observe today: that what the human ear perceives of sound is a specific range of physical vibrations—between sixteen thousand and thirty-two thousand per second—and that what lies beyond this on both the lower and higher ends is beyond human hearing, though other animals or some of them may perceive it.

Scientists researching animals have discovered wondrous understanding and subtle perception in types of animals such as the horse, dog, monkey, bear, bee, ant, and others—matters that are hardly found in many individual humans.

It has become clear from the preceding that the apparent context is that birds have a speech which Allah taught to Solomon. This exposes the error of those who claimed that the speaking of the birds was a miracle for Solomon, while they themselves possess no speech.

His saying: وَأُوتِينَا مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — **"and we have been given of everything"** (27:16). That is, we were given of everything. Although "everything" (كُلِّ شَيْءٍ) encompasses all that can be conceived as existing—since the concept of "thing" (شيء) is among the most general concepts and is preceded here by a word of universal inclusion (الاستغراق)—the context is one of recounting blessings. Not every blessing, but those blessings that a human can be given and enjoy. Thus, the meaning of "everything" is restricted by this, and the meaning of the sentence is: Allah has given us a significant portion of every blessing that a human can be given and enjoy, such as knowledge, prophethood, kingship, judgment, and other spiritual and material blessings.

His saying: إِنَّ هَذَا لَهُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْمُبِينُ — **"This is indeed a manifest favor"** (27:16). This is an expression of gratitude and an emphasis on the recounting of the blessing, free from vanity, pride, or arrogance, as he attributed everything to Allah by saying: "we have been taught" (عُلِّمْنَا) and "we have been given" (أُوتِينَا).

Some have suggested that this sentence is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and not the speech of Solomon, but the context rejects this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَحُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمَانَ جُنُودُهُ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ وَالطَّيْرِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ — **"Solomon’s hosts were mustered before him—jinn, humans and birds—and they were set in order"** (27:17). Mustering (الحشر) is the gathering of people and bringing them out for a matter with urgency. Setting in order (الوزع) means restraining (المنع), and it is said to mean detaining. The meaning, as has been said, is: Solomon’s soldiers from the jinn, humans, and birds were gathered for him, and they were restrained from dispersing or mixing one group with another by pushing the first of them back to the last of them and keeping each in its place.

It is inferred from the verse that he had soldiers from the jinn and birds who traveled with him just as his soldiers from the humans did.

The word "mustering" (الحشر), the description of those mustered as "hosts" (جنود), and the context of the following verses all indicate that his soldiers were specific groups of jinn, humans, and birds, whether the word "from" (مِنَ) in the verse is for partitivity (التبعيض) or for clarification (البيان).

The author of *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* went to an extreme, claiming that the verse indicates that all jinn, humans, and birds were his soldiers and that he ruled the entire earth. He further claimed that Allah made the birds in his time rational and legally accountable (عقلاء مكلفين), and they returned after his time to how they were before. He said the same regarding the ant that spoke. He said in the commentary of the verse: "The meaning is that Allah made all these categories his soldiers, and they would not be so unless they acted according to his will, and they would not be so except with the intellect that makes legal accountability valid, or being like an adolescent nearing the age of accountability. Therefore, we say: Allah made the birds in his days among those who possess intellect, and the state of birds in our days is not like that, even if there are those among them whom Allah has inspired with the subtleties they specifically need or which Allah specified for the benefit of servants, like the bee and others." [End of quote].

The instances of arbitrary assertion (التحكم) in this are too obvious to require explanation.

The jinn were mentioned before the humans and birds because their subjugation and entry into obedience is more wondrous. Humans were mentioned after them, rather than birds—despite their subjugation also being wondrous—to maintain the pairing between jinn and humans.

**His saying, the Exalted:** حَتَّى إِذَا أَتَوْا عَلَى وَادِ النَّمْلِ — **"When they came to the Valley of Ants..."** (27:18). The word "until" (حَتَّى) marks the end-point of what is understood from the previous verse. The plural pronoun refers to Solomon and his hosts. The use of the preposition "upon" (عَلَى) with "coming" (الإتيان) is said to be because the coming was from above. The Valley of Ants (وادي النمل) is a valley in Syria, according to some; others say in the land of Ta'if, and others say in the furthest part of Yemen. Crushing (الحطم) means breaking.

The meaning is: When Solomon and his hosts traveled until they came upon the Valley of Ants, an ant spoke, addressing the other ants: "O ants! Enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts crush you"—meaning, lest they trample you with their feet—"while they are unaware." In this is evidence that they were traveling on the ground.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَتَبَسَّمَ ضَاحِكًا مِنْ قَوْلِهَا — **"Thereat he smiled, laughing at her words"** (27:19). It is said that smiling (التبسم) is less than laughing (الضحك); based on this, "laughing" here is used metaphorically to mean being on the verge of it.

There is no contradiction between his saying (peace be upon him): "We have been taught the speech of the birds" and his understanding of the ant's speech, as he did not negate his understanding of the speech of other animals or some of them, like the ant.

A group of exegetes assumed that his saying "We have been taught the speech of the birds" necessitated the negation of everything else. Thus, they strained to justify his understanding of the ant's words: sometimes by saying it was a unique incident; other times by assuming it was a winged ant and thus among the birds; thirdly by saying her speech was among the miracles of Solomon (peace be upon him); and fourthly by saying he did not hear a sound from her at all, but rather understood what was in the ant's soul through inspiration (إلهام) from Allah.

The previously mentioned meaning of animal speech dispels these illusions. Furthermore, the context of the verses alone is sufficient to refute them.

His saying: وَقَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلَى وَالِدَيَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَالِحًا تَرْضَاهُ — **"and he said, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which may please You’"** (27:19). Inspiration (الإيزاع) means to instill or prompt. He (peace be upon him) smiled, rejoicing and delighted by what Allah had blessed him with, leading him to this station—which is prophethood, knowledge of animal speech, kingship, and hosts of jinn, humans, and birds. So he asked Allah to inspire him to thank His blessing and to act in a way that pleases Him, the Glorified.

He made the gratitude for the blessing Allah bestowed upon himself specific to him, and for the blessing He bestowed upon his parents; for the blessing upon his parents is a blessing upon him in a way, as he is from them. Allah blessed his father David with prophethood, kingship, wisdom, the decisive judgment, and more. He blessed his mother by marrying her to the prophet David, granting her the prophet Solomon, and making her of the People of the House of Prophethood.

In his speech is evidence that his mother was among the people of the Straight Path (الصراط المستقيم) whom Allah has blessed (1), and they are one of the four groups mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ — **"those whom Allah has blessed: the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous"** (4:69).

His saying: وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَالِحًا تَرْضَاهُ — **"and that I may do righteous deeds which may please You"** is conjoined to his saying: "to give thanks for Your blessing." His request—"inspire me to act," etc.—is a matter of higher rank and loftier station than asking for success (التوفيق) in righteous deeds. For success works upon external causes by arranging them so they align with a person's felicity, whereas the inspiration (الإيزاع) he asked for is an internal call within the human toward felicity. Based on this, it is not far-fetched that the intended meaning is the revelation (الوحي) with which Allah honored Abraham and his progeny, as He reports: وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ — **"and We revealed to them the doing of good works"** (21:73), which is the support by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), as mentioned in the commentary of that verse.

His saying: وَأَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبَادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ — **"and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants"** (27:19). That is, make me of them. Since this righteousness (الصلاح) was not restricted to action, it refers to the righteousness of the essence (صلاح الذات)—the righteousness of the soul in its substance, by which it becomes prepared to receive any divine dignity.

It is well known that the righteousness of the essence is of higher rank than the righteousness of action. Thus, in his saying: "and that I may do righteous deeds which may please You, and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants," there is a progression in the request from the lower to the higher. The righteousness of action was attributed to his own doing and choice in a way, unlike the righteousness of the essence; therefore, he asked for the righteousness of the essence from his Lord, and did not ask for the righteousness of action itself, but rather that He inspire him to act.

In his substituting the request for the righteousness of the essence with the request to be admitted among His righteous servants, there is an indication of his asking for the gifts Allah specifically granted them, the most abundant of which is servanthood (العبودية), with which Allah described him in His saying: نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ — **"What an excellent servant! He was indeed penitent"** (38:30).

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَتَفَقَّدَ الطَّيْرَ فَقَالَ مَا لِيَ لَا أَرَى الْهُدْهُدَ أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ الْغَائِبِينَ — **"He reviewed the birds, and said, ‘Why is it that I do not see the hoopoe? Or is he absent?’"** (27:20). Al-Raghib said: Reviewing (التفقد) is taking care (التعهد), but the reality of *tafaqud* is seeking to know the loss of a thing, while *ta'ahhud* is seeking to know a previous commitment. The Exalted said: "He reviewed the birds." [End of quote].

He first asked, wondering at his own state, as he did not see the hoopoe among the birds—as if it were not expected of the hoopoe to be absent from his procession or to refrain from obeying his command—then he turned from that to asking about its absence.

The meaning is: What is the matter with me that I do not see the hoopoe among the birds accompanying my procession? Rather, has he become one of the absent?

**His saying, the Exalted:** لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا أَوْ لَأَذْبَحَنَّهُ أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّي بِسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ — **"I will surely punish him with a severe punishment, or I will surely behead him, unless he brings me a clear authority"** (27:21). The prefixed *lams* are for an oath, and "clear authority" (سلطان مبين) means a manifest proof. He (peace be upon him) judged the hoopoe with one of three outcomes: severe punishment or slaughter—in which lies its misery—or bringing a clear justification, in which lies its release and salvation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ فَقَالَ أَحَطْتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ وَجِئْتُكَ مِنْ سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ — **"He did not stay for long. Then he said, ‘I have come to know what you do not know, and I have brought you a certain report from Sheba’"** (27:22). The pronoun in "he stayed" (فَمَكَثَ) refers to Solomon, though it is possible it refers to the hoopoe; the former is supported by the preceding context and the latter by what follows. "Encompassing" (الإحاطة) means complete knowledge. His saying: "and I have brought you," etc., is in the position of an explanatory conjunction for his saying: "I have come to know," etc. **Sheba** (سبأ) was a city in Yemen and its capital at that time. A **report** (النبأ) is news of importance, and **certainty** (اليقين) is that which has no doubt.

The meaning is: Solomon stayed—or the hoopoe stayed—for a time that was not long, then it appeared and Solomon asked it about its absence and rebuked it. It said: I have encompassed knowledge of that which you have not encompassed, and I have brought you from Sheba an important report about which there is no doubt.

From this, it appears that there is omission and brevity in the verse. It has been said that in the hoopoe’s words "I have come to know what you do not know," there is a breaking of Solomon’s intensity (peace be upon him) regarding the severity he had shown.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنِّي وَجَدْتُ امْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ — **"I found a woman ruling over them, and she has been given everything, and she has a great throne"** (27:23). The pronoun in "ruling over them" (تَمْلِكُهُمْ) refers to the people of Sheba and its dependencies. His saying: "and she has been given everything" is a description of the vastness and greatness of her kingdom. This serves as the context indicating that "everything" in the verse means everything that is a requisite of a great kingdom—such as prudence, resolve, power, a wide realm, treasures, mobilized armies, and obedient subjects. He specifically mentioned her great throne from among these.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَجَدْتُهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ — **"I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah..."** (27:24). That is, they were among the sun-worshippers of the polytheists.

His saying: وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ — **"and Satan has made their deeds seem goodly to them"** is in the position of an explanatory conjunction for what preceded it, and it is also a preparation for his subsequent saying: "and has barred them from the way." For Satan’s beautifying of their deeds—which are their prostration and other acts of devotion—is what diverted and prevented them from the way of Allah, which is His worship alone.

In the absolute use of "the way" (السبيل) without attributing it to Him, the Exalted, there is an indication that it is the way determined as the way itself for the human by virtue of his primordial nature (الفطرة), and indeed for everything by virtue of the general creation.

His saying: فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ — **"so they are not guided"** is a derivation from their being barred from the way; for there is no way when one is barred from the way, and thus no guidance. So understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ الَّذِي يُخْرِجُ الْخَبْءَ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُخْفُونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ — **"so that they do not prostrate to Allah, who brings forth what is hidden in the heavens and the earth, and knows what you hide and what you proclaim"** (27:25). The standard reading is "that not" (أَلَّا)—with a doubled *lam*—composed of "that" (أن) and "not" (لا). It is an appositive (عطف بيان) for "their deeds" (أَعْمَالَهُمْ). The meaning is: Satan beautified for them that they should not prostrate to Allah. It is also said to be with an implied *lam* of causality, meaning: Satan beautified their misguidance for them so that they would not prostrate to Allah.

The **hidden** (الخبء), according to *Majma' al-Bayan*, is the concealed (المخبوء), which is that which something else has surrounded so as to prevent its perception. It is a verbal noun used as a description. That which Allah brings into existence, bringing it from non-existence into being, is of this status. [End of quote].

In His saying: "who brings forth what is hidden in the heavens and the earth," there is a metaphor (استعارة), as if things are hidden and covered under the layers of non-existence, and Allah brings them forth into existence one after another. Thus, naming creation after non-existence as "bringing forth the hidden" is close to naming it "origination" (الفطر) and describing Him, the Exalted, as the Originator of the heavens and the earth. *al-Fatru* is splitting, as if He splits non-existence to bring forth things.

It is possible to take the sentence literally without metaphor, but that requires an explanation in a place other than this. It is said that "the hidden" means the Unseen (الغيب) and bringing it forth means knowing it, but this is as you see.

His saying: "and knows what you hide and what you proclaim" is with the "t" (تُخْفُونَ) for direct address, meaning: He knows your secret and your public. Most read it with the "y" (يُخْفُونَ) for the third person, which is more likely.

The summary of the argument: They only prostrate to the sun instead of Allah to venerate it for the good effects and general management of the earthly world and others that Allah has placed in its nature. Yet Allah, who brought all things from non-existence into being and from the Unseen to the manifest—whereupon the system of management was established from its root, including the sun and its management—is more worthy of veneration and more deserving that prostration be made to Him. Furthermore, there is no meaning in worshipping that which has no awareness of it, and the sun has no awareness of their prostration, while Allah, the Glorified, knows what they hide and what they proclaim. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the one determined for prostration and veneration, and none other.

By this explanation, the reason for the connection of the following verse becomes clear: "Allah—there is no god except Him," etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ — **"Allah—there is no god except Him, the Lord of the Great Throne"** (27:26). This is part of the hoopoe’s speech, and it is in the position of explicitly stating the result of the previous implicit explanation and manifesting the truth against their falsehood. Therefore, he first brought the declaration of oneness (التهليل) indicating the monotheism of worship, then joined to it His saying: "the Lord of the Great Throne," indicating that the management of affairs terminates with Him. For the royal throne is the station where the reins of affairs gather and from which the decrees current in the kingdom issue.

In His saying: "the Lord of the Great Throne," there is another parallel with his saying in describing the Queen of Sheba: "and she has a great throne." Perhaps these words of the hoopoe were what prompted—or were among what prompted—Solomon (peace be upon him) to command that her throne be brought to him, so that every greatness might submit to the greatness of his Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ سَنَنْظُرُ أَصَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْكَاذِبِينَ — **"He said, ‘We shall see whether you have spoken the truth, or whether you are one of the liars’"** (27:27). The pronoun refers to Solomon (peace be upon him). He deferred judgment in the hoopoe’s matter to the future; he did not believe it in its claim due to the lack of evidence for it yet, nor did he call it a liar due to the lack of evidence for its lying. Rather, he promised to test and reflect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** اذْهَبْ بِكِتَابِي هَذَا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَانْظُرْ مَاذَا يَرْجِعُونَ — **"Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then leave them and see what [answer] they return"** (27:28). This recounts Solomon’s speech addressing the hoopoe. It is as if it were said: Solomon wrote a letter and then said to the hoopoe: Take this letter of mine to them—meaning to the Queen of Sheba and her assembly—and cast it to them, then turn away from them—meaning move aside from them and stay in a place where you can see them—and see what they return—meaning what answer they return to one another when they speak about it.

His saying: "and deliver it" (فَأَلْقِهْ) is with a silent "h" in both joining and pausing in all readings, which is the "h" of silence (هاء السكت). Among what has been said regarding the verse is that His saying "then leave them and see," etc., is a case of transposition (التقديم والتأخير), and the original is: "and see what they return, then leave them." This is as you see.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَتْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتَابٌ كَرِيمٌ إِنَّهُ مِنْ سُلَيْمَانَ وَإِنَّهُ بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمَنِ الرَّحِيمِ — **"She said, ‘O elite! Indeed a noble letter has been delivered to me. It is from Solomon, and it begins with: "In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful"’"** (27:29-30). In the speech, there is omission and brevity. The estimation is: The hoopoe took the letter and carried it to the Queen of Sheba until, when he reached her, he cast it to her. She took it, and when she read it, she said to her assembly and the nobles of her people: "O elite!..." etc.

Her saying: "O elite! Indeed a noble letter has been delivered to me" recounts her mentioning the matter of the letter to her assembly, how it reached her, and its content. She magnified it by describing it as "noble" (كريم).

Her saying: "It is from Solomon, and it begins with: In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful" apparently serves as the reason for the letter being noble. That is, the reason for it is that it is from Solomon, and Solomon’s power and the great kingdom and wondrous might he was given were hardly hidden from her, as she acknowledged in her words recounted by Allah later: "and we were given knowledge before it, and we were Muslims."

"And it begins with: In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful"—meaning the letter is in His name, the Exalted, so it is noble for that reason. All polytheists believe in Allah, the Glorified, seeing Him as the Lord of lords even if they do not worship Him. The sun-worshippers among them—who are a branch of the Sabians—venerate Him and His attributes, even if they interpret the attributes as the negation of deficiencies and non-existence; they interpret knowledge, power, life, and mercy, for example, as the absence of ignorance, incapacity, death, and cruelty. Thus, the letter being in the name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the All-Merciful, necessitates its being noble, just as its being from the great Solomon necessitates its being noble. Based on this, the letter—meaning its content—is His saying: "Do not defy me, and come to me in submission," and the "that" (أن) is explanatory (مفسرة).

It is strange what is narrated from a group of exegetes: that her saying "It is from Solomon" is an initiation (استئناف) occurring as an answer to an implied question, as if it were said: "From whom is the letter and what is in it?" and she said: "It is from Solomon," etc. Based on this, her saying "and it begins with: In the Name of Allah" would be an explanation of the letter, meaning its text, and the letter would be: "In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful: Do not defy me, and come to me in submission."

Two objections face them: First, the occurrence of the word "that" (أن) as redundant with no benefit. Therefore, some of them said it is infinitival (مصدرية) and the "not" (لا) is negative, not prohibitive—which is a weak view, as will follow.

Second, explaining the reason for the letter being noble. It was said: the reason for its nobility is that it was sealed; in the hadith: "The honoring of a letter is its sealing," until some claimed that the meaning of a letter's nobility is its being sealed. It is said: "I honored the letter, so it is noble" when you seal it. It was also said: she called it noble for the quality of its script and the beauty of its expression. It was said: because it reached her in an extraordinary manner. It was said: because she thought, due to the bird casting it, that it was a heavenly book—among other views.

You are well aware that these are unconvincing arbitrary assertions. The apparent truth is that what led them into what they fell into was their taking His saying "and it begins with: In the Name of Allah" to His saying "in submission" as a recounting of the text of the letter. That contradicts taking His saying "It is from Solomon, and it begins with: In the Name of Allah," etc., as the reason for the letter's nobility. This is refuted by the fact that the "that" (أن) in His saying "Do not defy me," etc., is clearly explanatory, meaning it is a report of the meaning and content of the letter, not a recounting of its text. Thus, the result of the two verses is that the letter was begun with "In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful" and its content was the prohibition of defying him and the command to come to him in submission. There is no problem at all.

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَّ وَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ — **"‘Do not defy me, and come to me in submission’"** (27:31). The "that" (أن) is explanatory, explaining the content of Solomon’s letter, as previously indicated.

The claim of some that it is infinitival and the "not" is negative—meaning "your not defying me"—is weak because it necessitates, first, the estimation of a deleted subject or predicate without cause, and second, the conjunction of a performative (إنشاء)—which is His saying "and come to me"—to a report (إخبار).

What is intended by their "defying" (علوهم) him is their acting arrogantly toward him. By His saying "and come to me in submission" (مُسْلِمِينَ), it means their submission in the sense of compliance (الانقياد), as supported by His saying "Do not defy me," rather than submission in the technical sense, which is faith in Allah, the Glorified—even if their coming in compliance to him necessitates their faith in Allah, as inferred from the context of the hoopoe’s speech and the following verses. Had the technical meaning of faith been intended, it would have been appropriate to say: "Do not defy Allah."

The fact that Solomon (peace be upon him) was a prophet whose role was the call to Islam does not contradict this. For he was a messenger-king, and his call to absolute compliance necessitated that, as mentioned before. It eventually led to her submission to Allah, as Allah recounted from her: "and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds."

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَتْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي أَمْرِي مَا كُنْتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا حَتَّى تَشْهَدُونِ — **"She said, ‘O elite! Give me your opinion in my matter. I have never decided any matter until you were present’"** (27:32). Giving an opinion (الإفتاء) is manifesting a *fatwa*, which is a view. Deciding a matter (قطع الأمر) is judging it and resolving upon it. Presence (الشهادة) is being there. This is a consultation from her to them. She says: Advise me in this matter I have faced, which is indicated by Solomon’s letter. I only consult you in it because I have never, until today, been autocratic in my views on matters; rather, I judge and resolve based on your advice and presence.

The verse indicates a second part of her speech with her assembly, after the first part in which she informed them of Solomon’s letter, how it reached her, and what was in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالُوا نَحْنُ أُولُو قُوَّةٍ وَأُولُو بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَالْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَانْظُرِي مَاذَا تَأْمُرِينَ — **"They said, ‘We are a people of power and great might, but the matter rests with you; so see what you will command’"** (27:33). Power (القوة) is that by which one is strengthened toward a goal; here it refers to the army by which one is strengthened to repel the enemy and fight them. Might (البأس) is intensity in action, meaning valor and courage.

The verse contains the assembly’s response to her. They first let her hear what would please her soul and calm her anxiety, then they return the matter to her. They say: Be of good cheer and do not grieve, for we possess such power and intensity that we do not fear an enemy, even if it be Solomon. Then the matter is yours; command what you wish, for we are your obedient ones.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَتْ إِنَّ الْمُلُوكَ إِذَا دَخَلُوا قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوهَا وَجَعَلُوا أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَا أَذِلَّةً وَكَذَلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ — **"She said, ‘Indeed when kings enter a town, they corrupt it, and abase the most honored of its people. That is how they act’"** (27:34). Corrupting towns (إفساد القرى) is destroying them, burning them, and demolishing their buildings. Abasing the most honored of its people is through killing, capture, enslavement, exile, and domination.

Her view—as inferred from these two verses—was to gain further insight into Solomon’s matter (peace be upon him) by sending someone to him to test his state and witness the manifestations of his prophethood and kingdom, then report back to the Queen what he saw so she could resolve upon one of two things: war or peace. It was apparent from the assembly’s speech—since they began by saying "We are a people of power and great might"—that they leaned toward fighting. Therefore, she first began by disparaging war, then stated her view. She said: "Indeed when kings enter a town, they corrupt it," etc. That is, war only ends in the victory of one of the combatants, and in it is the corruption of towns and the humiliation of their honored ones. Thus, it is not prudent to proceed with it given the enemy’s power and might, as long as there is a way to peace and reconciliation, except out of necessity. My view is to send them a gift, then see what the messengers return with of news; at that point, I will decide on one of the two matters: war or peace.

So her saying: "Indeed when kings enter," etc., is a preparation for her subsequent saying: "I will send them a gift and see," etc.

Her saying: "and abase the most honored of its people" (وَجَعَلُوا أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَا أَذِلَّةً) is more eloquent and emphatic than saying, for example, "they humiliated its honored ones," because along with indicating the realization of humiliation, it indicates their becoming characterized by the attribute of humiliation.

Her saying: "That is how they act" (وَكَذَلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ) is intended to indicate continuity (الاستمرار) after the indication of the original occurrence in her saying: "they corrupt it, and abase the most honored of its people." It is said that the sentence is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and not the completion of the Queen of Sheba’s speech, but this is not sound, as there is no requirement in this context for such a confirmation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَإِنِّي مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِمْ بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَنَاظِرَةٌ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ الْمُرْسَلُونَ — **"I will send them a gift and see what the messengers bring back"** (27:35). That is, sending to Solomon. This is a kind of royal pride and self-importance; she guards her tongue from his name and attributes the matter to him and those with him together. Also, she indicates by this that he does what he does through the hands of his supporters, soldiers, and the aid of his subjects.

Her saying: "and see what the messengers bring back"—meaning, so that I may act then according to what the situation requires. This—as mentioned—is the view of the Queen of Sheba. It is known from the word "the messengers" (الْمُرْسَلُونَ) that the bearer of the gift was a group of her people, just as it is inferred from Solomon’s later saying "Go back to them" that there was a leader for the messengers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا جَاءَ سُلَيْمَانَ قَالَ أَتُمِدُّونَنِ بِمَالٍ فَمَا آتَانِيَ اللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا آتَاكُمْ بَلْ أَنْتُمْ بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ — **"So when he came to Solomon, he said, ‘Do you aid me with wealth? What Allah has given me is better than what He has given you. Rather you are the ones who boast of your gift’"** (27:36). The pronoun in "he came" refers to the wealth that was gifted to him or to the messenger who brought the gift.

The question in His saying: "Do you aid me with wealth?" is for rebuke (التوبيخ). The address is to the messenger and the sender by giving precedence to the one present over the one absent. The rebuking of the people without specifying the Queen among them is similar to her previous saying: "I will send them a gift," as we pointed out.

It was allowed that the address be to the messengers, and they were a group, but this is an error. For the aiding was not from the messengers but from the one who sent them; thus, there is no meaning in directing the rebuke to them specifically. The indefinite form of "wealth" (بِمَالٍ) is for belittlement (التحقير). What is intended by "what Allah has given me" is kingship and prophethood.

The meaning is: Do you aid me with a paltry wealth that has no value to me compared to what Allah has given me? For what Allah has given me of prophethood, kingship, and riches is better than what He has given you.

His saying: "Rather you are the ones who boast of your gift" is a transition from rebuking their aiding him with wealth to rebuking their joy in their gift. That is, your aiding me with wealth that has no value to me compared to what Allah has given me is ugly, and your joy in your gift because you magnify it and are impressed by it is uglier.

It was said: what is intended by "your gift" is the gift that is given to you. The meaning: Rather, you rejoice in what is given to you of gifts because of your love for increasing wealth, whereas I do not value the wealth of this world. This is far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ارْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُمْ بِجُنُودٍ لَا قِبَلَ لَهُمْ بِهَا وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُمْ مِنْهَا أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ — **"Go back to them, for we will come to them with hosts they cannot withstand, and we will surely expel them from it, abased and humiliated"** (27:37). The address is to the leader of the messengers. The plural pronouns refer to the Queen of Sheba and her people. **Power** (القبل) means capacity. The pronoun in "from it" (بِهَا) refers to Sheba. His saying: "and they are humiliated" (وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ) is an emphasis of what preceded it. The *lams* in "we will come to them" and "we will surely expel them" are for an oath.

Since their substituting compliance with his command—which was His saying: "and come to me in submission"—with the sending of a gift apparently meant refraining from submission, he judged according to the situation that they were not submitting to him. So he threatened them with sending hosts they could not withstand. Therefore, he made the coming with hosts a consequence of the messenger’s return without conditioning it on their not coming in submission. He said: "Go back to them, for we will come to them," etc., and did not say: "Go back, and if they do not come to me in submission, we will come to them," etc., even if the meaning returns to that. For the sending of hosts and their expulsion from Sheba in a state of humiliation was conditioned upon it in any case.

The context testifies that he (peace be upon him) returned their gift to them and did not accept it from them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِي بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَنْ يَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ — **"He said, ‘O elite! Which of you will bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?’"** (27:38). This is speech he spoke after returning the gift and sending back the messengers. In it is his informing that they will come to him in submission. He only wanted the bringing of her throne before her presence and that of her people with him so that it might be a manifest evidence of the reach of the power gifted to him by his Lord, and a dazzling miracle for his prophethood, so that they might submit to Allah just as they submit to him. This is inferred from the following verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِنَ الْجِنِّ أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَقُومَ مِنْ مَقَامِكَ وَإِنِّي عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِيٌّ أَمِينٌ — **"An afreet from among the jinn said, ‘I will bring it to you before you rise from your place [of judgment]. Indeed I am strong and trustworthy for it’"** (27:39). An **afreet** (عفريت)—as has been said—is a rebellious, wicked one. His saying: "I will bring it to you" (آتِيكَ) is either a subject noun (اسم فاعل) or a present tense verb of "bringing." The former is more appropriate for the context as it indicates being engaged in the act and is more suitable for the conjunction of His saying: "Indeed I am... for it," etc., which is a nominal sentence. So it was said.

His saying: "Indeed I am strong and trustworthy for it"—the pronoun refers to the bringing. That is, I am strong for the bringing of her throne; its carrying is not heavy for me, nor does its transport exhaust me. I am trustworthy; I will not betray you in this matter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ الَّذِي عِنْدَهُ عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ أَنَا آتِيكَ — **"The one who had knowledge of the Book said, ‘I will bring it...’"** (27:40). His being contrasted with the one before him is evidence that he was from the humans. Narrations have been transmitted from the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) that he was Asaf ibn Barkhiya, Solomon’s vizier and his successor (وصيه). It was said: he is al-Khidr. It was said: a man who had the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) which, if He is asked by it, He answers. It was said: Gabriel. It was said: he is Solomon himself. These are views for which there is no evidence.

Whoever he was and whatever he was, the speech was separated from what preceded it without being conjoined to it to show regard for the status of this scholar who brought her throne to him in less than the twinkling of an eye. Regard was also shown for the status of his knowledge, as it was used in the indefinite form: "knowledge of the Book" (عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ)—meaning a knowledge that words cannot describe.

What is intended by "the Book" (الكتاب), which is the source of this wondrous knowledge, is either the genus of heavenly books or the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ). The knowledge this scholar took from it was a knowledge that facilitated for him the attainment of this goal. Exegetes have mentioned that he knew the Greatest Name of Allah which, if He is asked by it, He answers. Some mentioned that this name is "the Ever-Living, the All-Sustaining" (الحي القيوم). It was said: "the Lord of Majesty and Honor" (ذو الجلال والإكرام). It was said: "Allah, the All-Beneficent." It was said: it is in Hebrew *Ahiya Sharahiya*. It was said: he supplicated by saying: "O our God and the God of everything, one God, there is no god except You, bring me her throne." Among other things said.

It was previously mentioned in the discussion on the Most Beautiful Names in the eighth volume of this book that it is impossible for the Greatest Name—which has the power of disposal over everything—to be of the nature of words, nor the concepts that words indicate and reveal. Rather, if there is a name that has this status or part of this status, it is the external reality of the name (حقيقة الاسم الخارجية) to which the concept of the word applies in a certain way. That is the name in reality, and the word indicating it is the "name of the name."

Nothing was mentioned in the wording of the verse regarding this name they mentioned. Rather, what the verse contains is that he had "knowledge of the Book" and that he said: "I will bring it to you." It is known, nonetheless, that the act is the act of Allah in reality. From all this, it is concluded that he had such knowledge of Allah and connection to Him that if he asked his Lord for something by turning to Him, He would not fail to respond. Or if you wish, say: if Allah, the Glorified, willed it.

It also becomes clear from the preceding that this knowledge was not of the category of discursive sciences (العلوم الفكرية) that accept acquisition and learning.

His saying: أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ — **"‘I will bring it to you before your gaze returns to you’"** (27:40). The **gaze** (الطرف)—as has been said—is the glance and looking. The return of the gaze is the arrival of the thing looked at to the soul and the human’s knowledge of it. So the meaning is: I will bring it to you in less than the temporal interval between looking at a thing and knowing it.

It was said: the gaze is the movement of the eyelids and their opening for looking, and its return is their closing. Because it is a natural matter not dependent on intention, "returning" (الارتداد) was preferred over "sending back" (الرد); so it was said: "before your gaze returns to you" and not "before you send back your gaze." This is what was said.

But he erred. For the gaze, like breathing, is among the human’s voluntary acts (أفعال الإنسان الاختيارية), except that what prompts it is nature, as in breathing. Therefore, it does not require a prior deliberation (ترو) for its occurrence, as is required for things like eating and drinking. A voluntary act is that which is linked to the human’s will, and it is broader than that which is preceded by deliberation. What led this speaker into what he fell into was his assumption of equality between an act proceeding from choice (اختيار) and one proceeding from deliberation. Perhaps the subtle point in preferring "returning" over "sending back" is that the act, due to its not depending on deliberation, is as if it occurs by itself, not from a volition of the observer.

The address in His saying: "I will bring it to you before your gaze returns to you" is to Solomon (peace be upon him). He is the one who wants it brought to him, and he is the one to whom it is intended to be brought.

It was said: the address is to the afreet who said: "I will bring it to you before you rise from your place." The one who had knowledge of the Book, according to this speaker, is Solomon. He only said it to him to manifest the virtue of prophethood, and that what Allah empowered him with by teaching him a knowledge from the Book is greater than the power the afreet boasted of. So the meaning: Solomon said to the afreet when he said what he said: I will bring you the throne before the return of your gaze.

The author of *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* insisted on this view and cited points to support it, but they are poor points, and the original view does not suit the context, as we have alluded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِنْدَهُ قَالَ هَذَا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي — **"So when he saw it set before him, he said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord...’"** (27:40) to the end of the verse. That is, when Solomon saw the throne set before him, he said: This—meaning the presence of the throne and its being set before me in less than the twinkling of an eye—is from the favor of my Lord, without any merit from me, "to test me"—meaning to examine me—"whether I give thanks" for His blessing "or am ungrateful." "And whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake"—meaning its benefit returns to him, not to my Lord. "And whoever is ungrateful"—and does not give thanks—"then my Lord is indeed All-sufficient, All-generous." In the end of the speech is an emphasis of what was in its beginning regarding the mention of favor.

It was said: the thing pointed to by "this" (هَذَا) is the empowerment to bring it, whether through an intermediary or by himself.

In this is the fact that the apparent sense of His saying: "So when he saw it set before him, he said," etc., is that this praise is linked to the state of seeing. What exists in the state of seeing is the presence of the throne before him, not the empowerment to bring it, which had been realized for some time.

In the speech, there is omission and brevity. The estimation is: Solomon permitted him to bring it thus, so he brought it as he said: "So when he saw it set before him." In the omission of what was omitted is a profound indication of the speed of the act, as if there were no interval at all between his claim to bring it thus and his seeing it set before him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَالَ نَكِّرُوا لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنْظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِي أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ — **"He said, ‘Disguise her throne for her; let us see whether she is guided, or if she is one of those who are not guided’"** (27:41). He said in *al-Mufradat*: **Disguising** (تنكير) a thing in terms of meaning is making it such that it is not recognized. The Exalted said: "Disguise her throne for her." Defining it (تعريفه) is making it such that it is recognized. [End of quote].

The context indicates that Solomon (peace be upon him) only said this when the Queen of Sheba and her assembly intended to come to him, when they entered upon him. He only wanted by that to test her intellect, just as he wanted by the original bringing of it to manifest a dazzling sign of the signs of his prophethood to her. Therefore, he commanded the disguising of the throne, then made His saying "let us see whether she is guided," etc., a consequence of it. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَكَذَا عَرْشُكِ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ وَأُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ — **"So when she came, it was said [to her], ‘Is your throne like this?’ She said, ‘It seems to be the same. We were given knowledge before it, and we were Muslims’"** (27:42). That is, when the Queen came to Solomon (peace be upon him), it was said to her from Solomon’s side: "Is your throne like this?" which is a word of testing.

He did not say: "Is this your throne?" rather, the disguise was increased, so it was said: "Is your throne like this?" Thus, he asked about the similarity of her throne to this throne pointed to in its form and attributes. In this very sentence, there is a kind of disguise.

Her saying: "It seems to be the same" (كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ)—what she intended by it is that it *is* it. She only expressed it with the word of comparison (التشبيه) to guard against rashness and hastening to confirm without verification. Initial beliefs that have not been verified are often expressed through comparison.

Her saying: "We were given knowledge before it, and we were Muslims"—the pronoun in "before it" (قَبْلِهَا) refers to this sign, meaning the bringing of the throne, or to this state, meaning her seeing it after she came. The apparent context is that it is the completion of the Queen’s speech. When she saw the throne and was asked about its matter, she sensed that this was an allusion from them to what Allah had given Solomon of extraordinary power. So she answered by saying: "We were given knowledge before it," etc. That is, there is no need for this allusion and reminder; we already knew of his power before this sign or this state, and we were Muslims to Solomon, obedient to him.

It was said: her saying "We were given knowledge," etc., is from the speech of Solomon. It was said: from the speech of Solomon’s people. It was said: from the speech of the Queen, but the meaning is: we were given knowledge of the bringing of the throne before this state. These are all poor views.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ** "And what she used to worship besides Allah kept her from [the faith]. Indeed she belonged to a faithless people" (27:43). The term "keeping from" (الصد) denotes prevention and diversion. That which she was kept from was "submission to Allah" (الإسلام لله), which is what she would testify to when commanded to enter the palace, saying: **أَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (27:44). As for her statement in the preceding verse, "and we had submitted" (وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ), it refers to her submission and compliance to Solomon (عليه السلام).

This is what the context of the verses yields; other commentators have proposed different interpretations for the meaning of the verse, which we have set aside.

His saying: **إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ** "Indeed she belonged to a faithless people" serves as the rationale for her being kept [from the faith]. The meaning is: that which she used to worship besides Allah—namely the sun, as mentioned previously in the report of the hoopoe—prevented her from submitting to Allah. The reason for this was that she belonged to a disbelieving people and followed them in their disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: **قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ** "It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace’" (27:44) until the end of the verse. The palace (الصرح) refers to a castle or any elevated building; it also denotes an expansive, open area without a roof. A "deep pool" (اللجة) refers to a vast body of water. "Smoothed" (الممرد) is the passive participle of "to smooth" (التمريد), meaning to make level and sleek. "Crystal" (القوارير) refers to glass.

His saying: **قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ** "It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace’"—it is as if the speaker was one of Solomon's servants in the presence of Solomon himself, one of those who would guide her to enter his presence, as is the custom when kings and dignitaries visit their peers.

His saying: **فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَنْ ساقَيْها** "So when she saw it, she took it to be a deep pool, and bared her shanks." That is, when she saw the palace floor, she thought it was a deep pool of water because of the clarity of the glass, which resembled water. She bared her shanks by gathering her garments so that her hems would not get wet.

His saying: **قالَ إِنَّهُ صَرْحٌ مُمَرَّدٌ مِنْ قَوارِيرَ** "He said, ‘It is a palace paved with crystal.’" The speaker is Solomon, drawing her attention to the fact that it was not a pool of water, but rather a smoothed palace made of glass. When she witnessed the grandeur of Solomon's kingdom—having already witnessed the matter of the hoopoe, the rejection of the gift, and the bringing of her throne—she did not doubt that these were signs of his prophethood, achieved without mere worldly cunning or political maneuvering. At that moment, she said: **رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي** "My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself..."

His saying: **قالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "She said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’" She first appealed to her Lord by confessing her wrongdoing (الظلم), as she had not worshipped Allah from the beginning or from the moment she saw these signs. Then she testified to her submission (الإسلام) to Allah alongside Solomon.

In her saying: **وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ** "and I submit with Solomon to Allah," there is a transition (التفات) regarding the Exalted from the second person to the third person. The reason for this is the shift from a general faith in Allah—when she said, "My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself"—to explicit monotheism (التوحيد). She testifies that her submission to Allah is alongside Solomon, following the path of Solomon's submission, which is monotheism (التوحيد). She then reinforces this declaration by describing the Exalted as "the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين), meaning there is no lord for any part of the worlds other than Him. This is the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية), which necessitates the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة)—a concept that no polytheist accepts.

## (A Discussion on the Story of Solomon (عليه السلام))

**1. What is mentioned of his stories in the Quran:** Only a small portion of his stories (عليه السلام) is mentioned in the Noble Quran; however, reflection upon them guides one to the general scope of his history and the manifestations of his noble personality.

Among these is his inheriting from his father, David. The Exalted said: **وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُودَ سُلَيْمَانَ** "And We gave Solomon to David" (38:30), and He said: **وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ** "Solomon inherited from David" (24:16).

Another is the granting of a magnificent kingdom to him, the subjugation of the jinn, birds, and wind for him, and his being taught the speech of birds. The mention of these blessings is repeated in the speech of the Exalted, as in Surah al-Baqarah (2:102), al-Anbiya (21:81), al-Naml (27:16-18), Saba (34:12-13), and Sad (38:35-39).

Also included is the reference to the casting of a [lifeless] body upon his throne, as in Surah Sad (38:34).

Another is the reference to the display of the "well-bred horses" (الصافنات الجياد) before him, as in Surah Sad (38:31-33).

Another is the reference to his being given understanding of the judgment regarding the sheep that strayed into the tilth, as in Surah al-Anbiya (21:78-79).

Another is the reference to the account of the ant, as in Surah al-Naml (27:18-19).

Another is the story of the hoopoe and the subsequent account of his dealings with the Queen of Sheba in Surah al-Naml (27:20-44).

Finally, there is the reference to the manner of his death (عليه السلام), as in Surah Saba (34:14).

We have provided the discussion pertaining to each of these stories in the commentary on the verses referring to them throughout this book.

**2. His praise in the Quran:** His name (عليه السلام) appears in over ten places in the speech of the Exalted, who praised him extensively, calling him a "penitent servant." The Exalted said: **نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ** "What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (38:30). He described him with knowledge and judgment, saying: **فَفَهَّمْنَاهَا سُلَيْمَانَ ۚ وَكُلًّا آتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا** "We gave its understanding to Solomon, and to each We gave judgment and knowledge" (21:79). He also said: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا** "Certainly We gave David and Solomon knowledge" (27:15), and: **وَقَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ الطَّيْرِ** "and he said, ‘O people, we have been taught the speech of the birds’" (27:16). He counted him among the guided prophets, saying: **وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُونُسَ وَهَارُونَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ** "and Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon" (4:163), and: **وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ** "and Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon" (6:84).

**3. Mention of him (عليه السلام) in the Old Testament:** His story occurs in the First Book of Kings, where it elaborates on his retinue, the majesty of his affairs, the vastness of his kingdom, the abundance of his wealth, and the extent of his wisdom. However, it mentions none of his stories referred to in the Quran, except for the account that when the Queen of Sheba heard of Solomon's fame, his building of the House of the Lord in Jerusalem, and the wisdom granted to him, she came to him with many gifts, met him, and asked him questions to test him, which he answered, after which she returned (1).

The Old Testament speaks ill of him (عليه السلام), claiming (2) that toward the end of his life, he turned away from the worship of God to the worship of idols, prostrating to idols worshipped by some of his wives.

It also mentions that his mother was the wife of Uriah the Hittite, and that David (عليه السلام) fell in love with her and committed adultery with her. She became pregnant by him, and he plotted to have her husband, Uriah, killed until he was slain in battle. David then took her among his wives, and she became pregnant a second time and bore him Solomon.

__________________
(1) First Kings, Chapter 10.
(2) Second Samuel, Chapters 11 and 12.

The Noble Quran exonerates him (عليه السلام) from the first of these two accusations by the same means it exonerates all prophets—by explicitly stating their guidance and infallibility (العصمة). Regarding him specifically, it says: **وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَانُ** "Solomon was not faithless" (2:102).

As for the second accusation, he is exonerated by what the Quran narrates of his prayer when he heard the speech of the ant: **رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلى والِدَيَّ** "My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents" (27:19). We have explained in its commentary that this indicates his mother was among the people of the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) whom Allah has blessed—the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous.

**4. Narrations regarding his stories (عليه السلام):** The reports narrated concerning his stories, especially the story of the hoopoe and the subsequent accounts of the Queen of Sheba, mostly contain strange matters that find few parallels even in mythical legends. Sound reason rejects them, and definitive history belies them. The most exaggerated of these are narrated from the likes of Ka'b and Wahb.

They reached such a level of exaggeration as to narrate that he ruled the entire earth, that his reign lasted seven hundred years, and that all humans, jinn, wild beasts, and birds were his soldiers. They claimed that in his assembly, six hundred thousand chairs were placed around his throne, upon which sat thousands of prophets and hundreds of thousands of princes from among the humans and jinn.

They also claimed that the mother of the Queen of Sheba was a jinni, and that her foot was like the hoof of a donkey, which she would hide from onlookers until she bared her shanks to enter the palace and her secret was revealed. Regarding her power, they claimed she had four hundred kings under her command, each ruling a province, and each king had four hundred thousand fighters. She supposedly had three hundred ministers managing her kingdom and twelve thousand commanders, each with twelve thousand fighters under him—among other wondrous reports that we can only categorize as *Isra'iliyyat* and thus disregard (1).

__________________
(1) Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the collections of narrations such as *al-Durr al-Manthur*, *al-Ara'is*, *al-Bihar*, and the lengthy commentaries.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Ihtijaj*, Abdullah ibn al-Hasan narrated with his chain from his forefathers (عليه السلام): When Abu Bakr resolved to prevent Fatimah (عليه السلام) from [possessing] Fadak and this reached her, she came to him and said: "O son of Abi Quhafah! Is it in the Book of Allah that you inherit from your father while I do not inherit from mine? You have brought a monstrous thing! Have you intentionally abandoned the Book of Allah and cast it behind your backs when it says: **وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ** 'Solomon inherited from David'?" — the tradition continues.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ** "so they were held in check" (27:17), he said: "The first of them were held back for the last of them."

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: "The word 'waiting' (الناظرة) in some dialects means 'expecting' (المنتظرة). Have you not heard His saying: **فَناظِرَةٌ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** 'and see what the envoys bring back' (27:35)?"

In *al-Basa'ir*, with its chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), he said: "Indeed, the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) consists of seventy-three letters. Asaph possessed only one letter of it. He spoke it, and the earth between him and the throne of Bilqis sank away, then he reached out and took the throne with his hand, and the earth returned to its original state faster than the blink of an eye. We possess seventy-two letters of the Name, and one letter remains with Allah, which He has reserved for Himself in the knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب). And there is no power or strength except by Allah, the All-High, the All-Great."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), and it is narrated in *al-Kafi* from Jabir from Abu Ja'far, and from al-Nawfali from Abu al-Hasan, the Master of the Military (عليه السلام).

The statement that "the Greatest Name is such-and-such letters and Asaph had one letter which he spoke" does not contradict what we previously established—that this Name is not of the nature of spoken words. The very context indicates that "letter" (الحرف) refers to something other than a phonetic letter; it is expressed this way because the common understanding of a "name" among people is a verbal name composed of spoken letters.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ** "before your glance returns to you" (27:40), several views were mentioned—until he said—the fifth is that the earth was "folded" (طويت) for him, and this is what is narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام).

**I say:** What is narrated regarding "folding" (الطي) is not different from what was previously narrated regarding "sinking" (الخسف).

The other views he cited were five: First, that the angels carried it to him. Second, that the wind carried it. Third, that Allah created successive movements in it. Fourth, that it broke through its place there and then emerged before Solomon. Fifth, that Allah caused it to cease to exist in its place and recreated it in Solomon's assembly.

There is another view mentioned by some: that existence is the "renewal of likes" (تجدد الأمثال) through His creation; Allah bestowed existence upon her throne in Sheba, and then in the following instant, He bestowed it in the presence of Solomon. These views range from the impossible—such as the fifth—to those for which there is no evidence, like the rest.

In the same source, al-Ayyashi narrated in his *Tafsir* with a chain: Musa ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Musa and Yahya ibn Aktham met, and the latter questioned him. Musa said: "I entered upon my brother, Ali ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام), and we exchanged exhortations until I reached the point of obedience to him. I said to him: 'May I be your ransom! Ibn Aktham asked me questions for which I should provide a verdict.' He laughed and said: 'Did you give him a verdict?' I said: 'No.' He asked: 'Why?' I replied: 'I did not know them.' He said: 'What are they?' I said: 'He said: Tell me about Solomon—was he in need of the knowledge of Asaph ibn Barkhiya?'" Then I mentioned the other questions.

He said: "Write, my brother: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. You asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted, in His Book: **قَالَ الَّذِي عِندَهُ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ** 'The one who had knowledge of the Book said' (27:40). That was Asaph ibn Barkhiya. Solomon was not incapable of knowing what Asaph knew, but he wished for his community of jinn and humans to know that Asaph was the Proof (الحجة) after him. That was from the knowledge of Solomon which he entrusted to Asaph by the command of Allah, so Allah gave him understanding of it so that there would be no dispute regarding his leadership (الإمامة) and his status as a guide—just as Solomon was given understanding during the life of David so that his leadership and prophethood would be recognized after him, to reinforce the Proof (الحجة) over creation."

**I say:** This narration was cited in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* from *al-Majma'*, after which the author said: "It is as you see," meaning he saw no merit in it. I see no reason for his objection other than that he encountered the mention of leadership (الإمامة) in it and it did not please him.

In *Nur al-Thaqalayn*, from *al-Kafi*, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), he said: "Be more hopeful for that which you do not expect than for that which you do expect..."—until he said—"and the Queen of Sheba went forth and submitted with Solomon (عليه السلام)."
